summary: Small `Tree` optimization

description.text:
`draw_item` is recalculating the height of an item on drawing every cell, when the result is always the same. This is done to skip drawing cells if they are out of visible `y` positions due to scrolling. However, the loop can be entirely avoided by calculating it before, once, and skipping the cell drawing.

I did a rudimentary test with a scene with only a `Tree` and this code:
```gdscript
extends Tree

func _ready() -> void:
	create_item()
	var item : TreeItem
	for i in 50000:
		item = create_item()
		item.set_text(0, "Tree")
	scroll_to_item(item)
	

func _process(_delta: float) -> void:
	queue_redraw()
	print(Performance.get_monitor(Performance.TIME_FPS));
```

The `fps` stabilized around 24/25 with this PR, while on `master` it was around 18/19, with these specs:
 - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13500 (20 threads) - 31.78 GiB memory

summary: Implement alternative transparency sorting modes

description.text:
Closes https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12967

This pr adds the basic framework to implement alternative heuristics to sort transparent meshes and makes use of it by adding two new sorting modes.

This pr slightly differs from the proposal by adding an extra default sorting mode `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_DEFAULT` and `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_ORTHOGONAL`. The default mode can be changed via a project setting or methods on the `RenderingServer`. 

Unlike was originally mentioned in the proposal, this should play nicely with https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109388, so there's no need to wait for it (it should still be merged since consistent mesh sorting would still benefit projects benefitting from this pr).

## Features implemented ##
New enum `TransparencySortMode` on the `RenderingServer` with 4 options:
- `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_DEFAULT`: Sorted by the default heuristic. (Default heuristic is TRANSPARENCY_SORT_DEPTH if unchanged)
- `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_DEPTH`: Sorted based on depth. (Current behavior)
- `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_ORTHOGRAPHIC`: Sorted based on dot product between a camera normal and a mesh position. (Identical to how orthographic camera sorts, but now possible with perspective cameras)
- `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_CUSTOM_AXIS`: Sorted based on a dot product between a custom `Vector3` axis and a mesh position.

Two new project settings under camera category to interact with the default sorting:
- `rendering/camera/transparency_sort/default_transparency_sort_axis` (default Vector3(0, 0, 1))
- `rendering/camera/transparency_sort/default_transparency_sort_mode` (default 1, i.e. `TRANSPARENCY_SORT_DEPTH`)

Two new `Camera3D` properties to override default sorting (`alpha_sort_axis` is only visible in editor when heuristic is set to sort by axis):
- `transparency_sort_axis` (default Vector3(0, 0, 1))
- `transparency_sort_heuristic` (default RenderingServer.TRANSPARENCY_SORT_DEFAULT)

Four new `RenderingServer` methods to control sorting:
```python
void camera_set_transparency_sort_axis(camera: RID, axis: Vector3)
void camera_set_transparency_sort_mode(camera: RID, mode: TransparencySortMode)
void set_default_transparency_sort_axis(axis: Vector3)
void set_default_transparency_sort_mode(heuristic: TransparencySortMode)
```

summary: CanvasItemAddTriangleArray: Errors when using bones

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: v4.4.1-stable, v4.1.1-stable
- Not reproducible in: v3.6.1-stable

### System information

- Godot v4.4.1.stable.mono - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+), Vulkan (Mobile), and OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1660 SUPER (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-10400F CPU @ 2.90GHz (12 threads)

### Issue description

When calling `canvas_item_add_triangle_array()` with `numberOfVertices * 4` length arrays for `bones` and `weights`, Godot reports the following errors:

**Compatibility Renderer**
Once:
> custom_shape.gd:40 @ _draw(): _draw(): Condition "base_offset != stride" is true. Returning: 0
>   <C++ Source>  drivers/gles3/rasterizer_canvas_gles3.cpp:2548 @ request_polygon()
>   <Stack Trace> custom_shape.gd:40 @ _draw()

Once per frame:

> _render_batch: Parameter "pb" is null.
>   <C++ Source>  drivers/gles3/rasterizer_canvas_gles3.cpp:1333 @ _render_batch()

**Mobile and Forward+ Renderers**
Once:
> custom_shape.gd:40 @ _draw(): Condition "base_offset != stride" is true. Returning: 0
>   <C++ Source>  servers/rendering/renderer_rd/renderer_canvas_render_rd.cpp:294 @ request_polygon()
>   <Stack Trace> custom_shape.gd:40 @ _draw()

Once per frame:
> _render_batch: Parameter "pb" is null.
>   <C++ Source>  servers/rendering/renderer_rd/renderer_canvas_render_rd.cpp:3070 @ _render_batch()


### Proposed Solution
"drivers/gles3/rasterizer_canvas_gles3.cpp:2548" and "servers/rendering/renderer_rd/renderer_canvas_render_rd.cpp:294" both share the code: `ERR_FAIL_COND_V(base_offset != stride, 0);` My untested belief is that `base_offset` is two smaller than `stride` due to the following code (all comments are my own!):
```c++
// Lines 122 and 2437
if ((uint32_t)p_bones.size() == vertex_count * 4 && (uint32_t)p_weights.size() == vertex_count * 4) {
	stride += 4;
}

// Later, in lines 228 and 2508:
if ((uint32_t)p_indices.size() == vertex_count * 4 && (uint32_t)p_weights.size() == vertex_count * 4) {
	// {Do stuff}
	base_offset += 2; // This increase being two smaller is expected and not part of the issue; base_offset was already increased by two earlier.
}
```
The issue is in the if statements: 
- stride is increased on the condition `p_bones.size() == vertex_count * 4` 
- base_offset is increased on the condition `p_indices.size() == vertex_count * 4`.

While I'm still trying to figure out how bones work with this function, I don't believe there's any reason for bones to only be assigned when the mesh has exactly 1.3333 triangles per vertex? I think the second if statement should check if the number of bones is equal to 4 times the vertex count to match the check previous in the function (as well as elsewhere in the engine).

When passing in an indices array with vertex*4 entries, the errors do not appear.

### Steps to reproduce

```gdscript 
func _draw() -> void:
  var indicies = [0, 1, 2]
  var vertices = [Vector2(0,50), Vector2(25,0), Vector2(50,50)]
  var colors = [Color(1,1,1), Color(1,1,1), Color(1,1,1)]
  var uvs = [Vector2(0,1), Vector2(0.5,0), Vector2(1,1)]
  var bones = [0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2]
  var weights = [0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5]
  RenderingServer.canvas_item_add_triangle_array(get_canvas_item(), indicies, vertices, colors, uvs, bones, weights)
```
Note: I have no idea what is supposed to be in the bones and weights arrays, only that they need to be four times the size of the vertices array. For the purposes of this bug, I believe it only matters that these values are present and that their specific values are not important.


Regarding the MRP: when ran, you should see a white triangle in the top-left corner. In the inspector or code for "Node2D", change `_enable_bones` to `true` and observe the errors. If you also enable `_enable_weird_indicies`, the errors will disappear. 

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[trianglearraybonesbug.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21765806/trianglearraybonesbug.zip)

summary: Use R/F keys for local up/down and Space/Ctrl for global up/down

description.text:
This PR aligns the Godot editor's free look camera movement controls and features with that of other 3D software. Unreal Engine already uses R/F for local up/down.

In master, the E and Q keys are configurable. By default they perform local up/down movement, but if you change  `editors/3d/freelook/freelook_navigation_scheme`, they can be global. This default is the same as the behavior of Unity. Also, both Unity and Godot currently do not have any dedicated controls to move the camera globally up/down, only locally, unless changing that editor setting, but then it's only globally until you change it back.

This PR adds a new dedicated binding for always local up/down, R and F keys, and adds a new feature, global up/down movement, to the Space and Ctrl keys. These features are found in most 3D software, though not always the same keys.

In summary, the controls now look like this:

* R and F: Always local up/down.
* Space and Ctrl: Always global up/down.
* E and Q: Configurable up/down based on `editors/3d/freelook/freelook_navigation_scheme` (kept same as master).

This also matches 6DoF games such as Elite: Dangerous and Pioneer which use R and F for local up/down, and while 6DoF games don't typically need global up/down, they use E and Q for rolling instead. If Godot ever gets a 6DoF editor camera (which would be nice actually, but that's a separate discussion), this allows the R and F keys to act as local up/down movement for both, with E and Q being rolling the camera in the 6DoF mode.

Note that these keys are only active when holding right-click, so this doesn't conflict with F to focus or R to scale, in the same way that the Q/W/E keys already don't conflict with the select/move/rotate toolbar hotkeys.

Old PR description:

<details>

This PR changes local up/down to use the R and F keys, and adds a new feature, global up/down movement, to the E and Q keys. These bindings match the behavior in most 3D software, such as Unreal Engine and Blender's fly/walk navigation. This behavior is a superset of what Unity and older Godot versions have, but with different key bindings.

This also matches 6DoF games such as Elite: Dangerous and Pioneer which use R and F for local up/down, and while 6DoF games don't typically need global up/down, they use E and Q for rolling instead. If Godot ever gets a 6DoF editor camera (which would be nice actually, but that's a separate discussion), this allows the R and F keys to act as local up/down movement for both, with E and Q being either global up/down or rolling the camera.

If desired, I have another branch that only adds the R/F key bindings but leaves E/Q unchanged, and I could open a pull request for that on older Godot branches so that R/F works in all recent versions of Godot.

</details>

summary: Add Stretch Modes for Splash Screen

description.text:
Rework of #56959

Its been a few years since that PR has been modified (originally made for 4.0) so I started again with 4.5, solved any conflicts with the previous one, and made some additions with how new platform specific splash screens are handled.

To rehash, this will replace the `application/boot_splash/fullsize` project setting that can only be toggled on or off in favor of various stretching options that change how the boot image will appear for Godot projects on startup. 

It's a full upgrade because the stretching options include what toggling full size on or off meant previously and with the new options. However, this mostly applies to the editor boot splash and exported desktop platforms because mobile platforms have their own platform-specific approaches for showing boot splashes.

The new setting is called `application/boot_splash/stretch_mode` and the options include:

* *Disabled* (same as toggling the legacy full size project setting off, no stretching applied)
* *Keep* (same as toggling the legacy full size project setting on, stretches while preserving aspect ratio)
* *Keep Width* (stretches width based on height)
* *Keep Height* (stretches height based on the width)
* *Cover* (stretches until it covers the entire screen while preserving aspect ratio)
* *Expand* (stretches until it covers the entire screen but doesn't preserve aspect ratio)

To preserve compatibility, `set_boot_splash` retains the same signature but now routes to a new function `set_boot_splash_with_stretch` which is what's now used for the various renderers. Also, for projects that still have the legacy project setting serialized, a check is done to convert that to the corresponding stretch option.

summary: Implement screen-space based selection outline as default alternative to AABB

description.text: Closes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/11990 Closes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/2795 Begins to Address: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/109223#issuecomment-3173070375 Requires and Includes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/104233 (because I am dumb and often forget I'm working on a complex fork and this PR borrowed some functions from a different PR that has already been approved) Full disclosure I'm not a rendering expert by any means. I tried a stencil-buffer approach as suggested in the first linked proposal, but it ended up being complex and I couldn't get it working quite right especially when trying to select instantiated scenes with several complex meshes and handling an `active node` (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/102176). I think we would need to expose the stencil buffer as a post-process texture to get ideal results which is way beyond my skills set, so I opted to implement something more simple which is just do some post processing in screen-space with a color buffer. Reverting back to using AABB is done by checking the enable button in 3D editor settings. They also have their own color settings to maintain compatibility. summary: Defer `format_changed` and `frame_changed` signal for all camera feeds

description.text:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/contributing/development/code_style_guidelines.html
-->
When creating camera feed drivers for Godot, most of the time the pixel data of camera devices comes from threads outside Godot. Dire things might happen when users try connecting `frame_changed` signal called from non-Godot threads to do something such as accessing camera texture alongside with other GUI input signals (deadlock caused by deactivating camera feeds from BaseButton `pressed` signal while `frame_changed` still being emitted is what I've personally experienced).

This PR ensure `format_changed` and `frame_changed` signals are always emitted on Godot's main thread by deferring `emit_signal` of camera feeds. Not sure if this change is the correct way to do, but it does fix my deadlock problem.

summary: `SubtweenTweener::step` processes delta incorrectly

description.text: ### Tested versions v4.4.1.stable.custom_build [d91ae7f7e] ### System information Godot v4.4.1.stable (d91ae7f7e) - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2070 SUPER (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6603) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-10700F CPU @ 2.90GHz (16 threads) ### Issue description This code in SubtweenTweener::step does not process delta correctly: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/8aa37cab8950351d96f541581abdc42136283f24/scene/animation/tween.cpp#L899-L903 It's broken in two ways: 1. `subtween->step(r_delta)` returns true even when the subtween completes, so the if statement needs to actually be checking `!subtween->is_running()` 2. The math with `r_delta` checks out if `subtween->get_total_time()` reflected the true length of the subtween. However, the subtween's `total_time` is influenced by the same delta used to calculate `elapsed_time`. This means that `elapsed_time` will always be equivalent to the subtween's `total_time`, and therefore `r_delta` will always be set to 0. According to documentation, this is intended behavior: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/8aa37cab8950351d96f541581abdc42136283f24/doc/classes/Tween.xml#L143-L144 This could be fixed by making `get_total_time()` accurate to the time elapsed within a tween by making the change in this screenshot (though this is a behavior change that causes the NOTE in the above documentation to become inaccurate): --- This distinction leads to incorrect behavior in subtween steps -- essentially, delta does not carry through when a SubtweenTweeners completes in the same frame as its step, causing the Tweener to pause for a frame after subtweens at best, or completely breaking custom steps at worst. ### Steps to reproduce The MRP is a test scene with several tool buttons that shows the difference between running a tween that uses multiple subtweens VS. attempting to skip through them using `custom_step`. Applying the fixes described above results in correct behavior, though I'm unsure if we're comfortable changing how `get_total_time()` returns, even if it leads to an (arguably) more accurate result. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [subtween_step_bug_2025-08-13_15-46-38.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21762171/subtween_step_bug_2025-08-13_15-46-38.zip)

summary: Unused editor settings with no effect

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.5.beta5.official

### System information

N/A

### Issue description

This is a follow-up to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/100699.
As [reported by Nodragem](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/100699#issuecomment-2585289890), there are still some remaining editor settings with no effect since the GridMapEditor moved to the bottom panel. Additionally, I've found one more editor setting that is not referenced anywere.

summary: Improve panning behavior in 3D editor viewport with orthogonal projection

description.text:
Implements https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/11293.

This change improves the navigation feel when panning in 3D viewports using an orthogonal projection.

You can even set `editors/3d/navigation_feel/translation_inertia` to `0.0` to match the navigation feel of the 2D editor viewport.

# See it in action
The cursor no longer drifts away from the initial point (using the default editor settings):

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d097bea7-2cb1-448e-be71-c4405b51ee62

Panning speed is still influenced by the Maya NavigationScheme (SHIFT for a 10x speed multiplicator) and the `translation_sensitivity`, so the cursor can still drift away, but it now behaves in a more natural way.

summary: `@export` typed Arrays/Dictionaries with custom type shows incorrectly in the inspector when not using `class_name`

description.text:
### Tested versions

4.4.1

### System information

Windows 10

### Issue description

Premise:
Since GDScript doesn't feature namespaces, I'm trying to develop avoiding the use of class_name to pollute the project, and using `Script` preloading instead.

Using preloaded types in typed arrays or dictionaries results in different behaviour to using types defined with `class_name`.

Using `class_name` correctly shows the  `@export` variables of the custom type.
If you instead are preloading the script, the inspector will show the dropdown button of a generic `Resource`, and you can't create a new instance of your custom type from the inspector.

### Steps to reproduce

```gdscript
# MyDatabase.gd
extends Resource
const MyData : Script = preload("res://my_data.gd")
@export var dict : Dictionary[StringName, MyData]
```
If `MyData` is defined like this:
```gdscript
#my_data.gd
#class_name MyData
extends Resource
@export var a : int
```
If you uncomment `class_name MyData` the behaviour is as expected.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

WIP

MacOS

I am working with admob and I have had about 20 iterations where apps don't open because it cant find the AdMob node that is clearly there. Turns out changing the name of the parent node fixes it but still a very odd fix/issue. 

### Steps to reproduce

I create a new scene with a node as root. I add an AdMob node as a child to that node, and then make a script attached to the root node for some functionality with AdMob. summary: Cannot decode WebP images that are lossless and transparent

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in v4.5.beta5.official [c81fd6c51]- 
- Reproducible in v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6]
- Reproducible in v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee]

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable - Fedora Linux 42 (Workstation Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Mobile) - integrated AMD Radeon 760M Graphics (RADV PHOENIX) - AMD Ryzen 5 7640U w/ Radeon 760M Graphics (12 threads)

### Issue description

When importing a WebP image, if the image is lossless and has a transparent background, Godot fails to decode it. The editor returns the following errors on import:

```
ERROR: Failed decoding WebP image.
ERROR: core/io/image_loader.cpp:100 - Error loading image: 'res://Lossless transparent.webp'.
ERROR: Error importing 'res://Lossless transparent.webp'.

```
Additionally, when trying to open the asset, Godot returns the following errors:

```
ERROR: Failed loading resource: res://Lossless transparent.webp. Make sure resources have been imported by opening the project in the editor at least once.
ERROR: editor/editor_node.cpp:1361 - Condition "res.is_null()" is true. Returning: ERR_CANT_OPEN
```
By contrast, if the WebP image is exported lossy, or with an opaque background, Godot can read the image fine.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Download the test images, OR export a WebP image from Krita that has a transparent background. In the export settings, enable 'Lossless encoding' and set the quality to 100%. (The issue doesn't occur when I set quality to less than 100%).
2. Import the lossless, transparent WebP image into a new Godot project

[Test images.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21745174/Test.images.zip)

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[web-p-error.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21745214/web-p-error.zip)

### Related forum discussion
https://forum.godotengine.org/t/cant-import-transparent-lossless-webp/119084/6

summary: Speed up signal disconnects in the editor

description.text: In editor builds only, disconnecting signals is O(n) where n is the number of connections to that signal. The O(n) operation can easily be made O(1) by filtering by the relevant signal and connection. This is a problem in particular for large numbers of sibling `Controls`, as they all register `item_rect_changed` with their parent node. To see the problem in the test project: 1. Open `MeshInstance.tscn` 2. Open `Label25k.tscn` 3. Attempt to switch back to the `MeshInstance` tab by clicking on it Without the PR, step 3 hangs the editor for about ~23 seconds on my machine. With the PR, it takes a fraction of a second. When attempting to switch scenes, the way it frees the nodes causes all the disconnections to happen in sequence, which ends up being O(n^2) on 25,000 nodes. The `get_persistent_signal_connection_count` function is currently only used in editor code, isn't exposed to GDScript, and the new definition is backwards compatible with the old one, so I hope it's okay to change. Here's a flamegraph of the problem occuring during the repro steps: --------------------- All performance tests were run on builds made with the following command: `scons optimize=speed_trace debug_symbols=yes platform=linuxbsd dev_build=no production=yes`. The test project [editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21709465/editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip) consists of a series of scenes that each have 25,000 of a common type of node.

summary: Optimize CPU text shaping

description.text: I've been working on some editor optimizations, and ran into a couple places where text shaping ended up being a bottleneck. For instance, on editor startup (populating menu lists), or when switching to large scenes (`SceneTreeDock` calculating string heights). I optimized this by analyzing `SceneTreeDock` refreshes and editor launches to start with, and then built a custom testing scene so that reviewers could easily replicate the results. The project is just to replicate the bottlenecks I found in practice. In the test project there's a `TextServerOptimization.tscn` that consists of a Tree that constantly queues redraws of its 25000 everchanging elements. Here's the flamegraph for `_shape_lines` for that without this PR: And here's the flamegraph with this PR: We can compare before and after timings by using the amount of time spent in `hb_shape_full code` during shaping. Before this PR, 7.57% of shaping time was spent in `hb_shape_full` code. After this PR, 14.1% is. Since that function call wasn't changed and should be constant, that means that text shaping should be taking at most `(100 - 14.1) / 14.1 * 7.57 + 7.57 = 53.7%` of the time it was taking before. Put another way, in my tests CPU text shaping with this PR is 86% faster than on `master`! All of the changes fall into two categories: avoiding unnecessary dynamic allocations, and avoiding looking up font information we don't need to because the mutex checks aren't cheap even if it is cached. The `ScriptIterator`, `_font_is_script_supported`, and `reserve` changes are there to avoid allocations, while the cached font information and `FontPriorityList` are meant to avoid hitting the more expensive synchronized cache when we don't need to. The function level caching works well because fonts tend to be used in streaks, so it's mostly the same information getting looked up repeatedly. The `FontPriorityList` implements the same logic as before, but only ends up calling `_font_is_script_supported` and `_font_is_language_supported` on the minimum number of fonts needed. Thoughts on the `FontPriorityList` location and `friend` use is appreciated, I'm not sure what the preference is for that. Could move it to its own file, move the definitions to the .cpp, swap them to lambdas... --------------------- All performance tests were run on builds made with the following command: `scons optimize=speed_trace debug_symbols=yes platform=linuxbsd dev_build=no production=yes`. The test project: `TextServerOptimization.tscn` in [editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21709465/editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip)

summary: Speed up large selections in the editor

description.text:
There are many places where selections have `O(n^2)` behavior, likely because they're generally small enough for it not to matter. When trying to work with large scenes, however, I naturally found myself selecting 1000 or more elements at a time, and that's around the scale where that sort of behavior starts to blow up. 

I realize most people aren't going to be working with selections this large, but I don't see a good reason not to support it. Some of these changes slightly slow down small selections, some speed them up. I didn't bother testing whether small selections were sped up or slowed down on the net because they're still fast and infrequent enough for it not to matter.

I've split this PR in two commits to make it easier to review; I'll squash them once people are happy. About half the changes are just using references instead of copies, and it clutters up the actually interesting changes, so I split them out into their own commit.

Most of these changes are direct fixes to issues that showed up on a flamegraph while I was testing, so they do have actual impact. The exception is the swapping of copies for const references. After I ran into two instances that mattered, I just searched the entire codebase and fixed them all, so some of them probably don't matter in practice.

Most of the changes are just complexity class improvements that should be self explanatory. The `editor_data` change is a fix for what looks like a rather impactful typo. The two signal emission updates prevent doing work until the selection is done changing.

This speeds up many types of selections and deselections.

Selecting 5000 sprites via the scene tree dock: 6s -> ~0.25s
Deselecting 5000 sprites by selecting 1 new sprite in the scene tree dock: 4s -> imperceptible
Selecting 25000 sprites via the scene tree dock: 172s -> ~0.5s

There's similarly massive speedups to selection via drag select and to more types than just sprites, but you basically know what to expect from the above numbers.

---------------------

All performance tests were run on builds made with the following command: `scons optimize=speed_trace debug_symbols=yes platform=linuxbsd dev_build=no production=yes`.

The test project [editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21709465/editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip) consists of a series of scenes that each have 25,000 of a common type of node. Unless you also apply some of #109517, #109514, #109513, and #109512, it might be difficult to test anything but the `Sprite2D.tscn` scene.

summary: Miscellaneous editor optimizations for large scenes

description.text:
There were a few miscellaneous changes that didn't fit thematically into the other optimization PRs, so I've grouped them here.

#### `editor_node.cpp` transform flushing
The `editor_node.cpp` change is necessary because some editor plugins expect the transforms to be properly set up during the `_load_editor_plugin_states_from_config` call later. 

As a concrete example of an issue, open the `Mesh3DInstance25k.tscn` file. Opening it takes ages because of a convoluted path that triggers an issue in the `DynamicBVH` code. Because the instances all have the same local coordinates, they're initially loaded with identical transforms. They're added to the occlusion culler's dirty instance list, and then during the plugin setup something is `freed` from the culler, causing the dirty list to get processed. However, the transforms have never been propagated between scene loading and now, so everything is inserted at the same location. This triggers an `O(n^2)` edge case in the BVH.

I wouldn't have noticed this issue without it triggering a separate bug. However, both the official plugins and one user plugin end up doing twice as much setup work as they should, because they process the state of the world during the plugin, and then get all the transform updates later. Setting up the transforms properly first seems like the correct thing to be doing, anyways.

[Here's an example of a user plugin running into this issue.](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/94648#issuecomment-2263572842)

I'm not sure this is exactly the right place to put the `flush_transform_notifications` call, it could also be put inside `set_edited_scene`, but I erred on the side of the least impactful change.

#### `canvas_item_editor_plugin` EDITOR_GET removal
When I profiled the delay when selecting canvas items in the editor, literally 60% of the time was spent on this single `EDITOR_GET` call, so I cached it. The delay in question is small, but enough that it felt laggy to me, so I looked into where the time was being spent and found this.

#### `_node_visibility_changed` cache use
Perhaps there's a good reason not to do this, but I couldn't find any issues when testing it. It does dramatically improve the time to update the visibility of large numbers of objects at once.

1. Open `Sprite2D25k.tscn`
2. Select 25000 sprites
3. Toggle the visibility

On `master`, step 3 alone takes ~50 seconds. With this PR, it takes about a half a second.

If you want to test this particular speedup yourself, it'll likely be difficult without also applying #109515 so that large selections are less problematic.

---------------------

All performance tests were run on builds made with the following command: `scons optimize=speed_trace debug_symbols=yes platform=linuxbsd dev_build=no production=yes`.

The test project [editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21709465/editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip) consists of a series of scenes that each have 25,000 of a common type of node.

summary: Speed up deletion via the Scene Tree Dock in large trees

description.text: If you open `Label25k.tscn` in the test project below, and then attempt to delete one of the elements by selecting it in the scene tree dock and then pressing delete, it takes ~12 seconds for me without this PR. With this PR, it's less than half a second. Here's the flamegraph showing the problem: The `SceneTreeEditor` change take the performance from terrible to usable, getting the time down to under a second by itself. The `Tree` `Vector` -> `LocalVector` change only makes hundreds of ms worth of difference, but that's enough to bring it from usable to pretty good. It's the less important of the two, if it's objectionable. --------------------- All performance tests were run on builds made with the following command: `scons optimize=speed_trace debug_symbols=yes platform=linuxbsd dev_build=no production=yes` The test project [editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21709465/editor-optimization-test-scenes.zip) consists of a series of scenes that each have 25,000 of a common type of node.

summary: Make all `AudioStreamPlayer` type `volume_db` sliders cap out at 24dB, add `or_greater`

description.text:
This makes the `AudioStreamPlayer3D` `volume_db` have an upper limit of 24dB instead of 80dB, bringing it in line with the other stream players.

This also adds `or_greater` to all the stream players' `volume_db` sliders.

Closed https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109454 in favor of this.

As mentioned in the above PR, I believe this should be merged alongside (or before) https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109452, so people don't quickly drag up the `AudioStreamPlayer3D` dB volume sliders and explode their ears.

`or_greater` was originally added to keep compat, in case anyone had their 3D stream player `volume_db` sliders above 24. [It was suggested](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109454#issuecomment-3170652411) that `or_greater` be added to the other stream players, so I created this PR

EDIT: dang, I realized I named the branch very poorly. I hope that isn't a problem

summary: Add PipeWire camera feeds

description.text:
<!--
Please target the `master` branch in priority.

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/contributing/development/code_style_guidelines.html
-->
This PR uses PipeWire to provide camera feeds on Linux systems.

The benefits of using PipeWire for camera devices includes not having to use V4L2 kernel API directly, better integrated for desktop environments (camera indicator on KDE Plasma for example), and sandboxed camera access is also possible by using XDG desktop portal.

Camera feed driver on linuxbsd will be selected at runtime, and fallback to V4L2 driver if failed to initialize PipeWire.

Changes are committed separately for review, they will be squashed after approval.

TODO:

- [x] Use XDG desktop portal to request camera permission when setting `monitoring_feeds` to true (supersede #100396)
- [x] Use sowrap
- [x] Select PipeWire or V4L2 driver at runtime
- [x] Add support for planar YUV 420 format (Done but no hardware to test)
- [x] Test with more camera feed demo projects
 Tested with the following shader:
```gdshader
shader_type canvas_item;

const mat3 yuv2rgb_bt709 = mat3(
	vec3(1.00000, 1.00000, 1.00000),
	vec3(0.00000, -0.21482, 2.12798),
	vec3(1.28033, -0.38059, 0.00000)
);

uniform sampler2D texture_yuy2;

void fragment() {
	vec2 size = TEXTURE_PIXEL_SIZE;
	vec2 UV_u = UV - floor(mod(UV / size, 2)) * vec2(1, 0) * size;
	vec2 UV_v = UV + (vec2(1, 0) - floor(mod(UV / size, 2))) * vec2(1, 0) * size;
	float y = texture(texture_yuy2, UV).r;
	float u = texture(texture_yuy2, UV_u).g;
	float v = texture(texture_yuy2, UV_v).g;
	vec3 rgb = yuv2rgb_bt709 * vec3(y, u - 0.5, v - 0.5);
	COLOR = vec4(rgb, 1.0);
}
```
 Tested with [GDMP demo](https://github.com/j20001970/GDMP-demo) vision tasks without enabling camera feed addon.
 Tested with [godot-camerafeed-demo](https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo).

summary: [3.x] Windows export: surface rcedit failures (icon & PE metadata) with clear warnings/errors

description.text:
Description:

Fixes #27765.

Summary
This PR improves error reporting when running rcedit during Windows exports on Linux/macOS (via Wine) and Windows. Previously, failed rcedit calls (e.g. icon replacement or PE metadata) could be silent or misreported, leaving users with the default Godot icon and no actionable feedback. We now:

Validate configuration upfront and warn when Windows → Application fields or icon are set but rcedit is not configured. (Related: #57320.)

Capture exit code and stderr/stdout from the rcedit process (Wine included) and surface them in the export panel and CLI output.

Map non-zero exit codes to an explicit export error/warning, instead of generic messages like “templates missing” (see #28456), while still allowing export to proceed when safe.

Add small UX text tweaks guiding users to Editor Settings → Export → Windows → rcedit and docs.

Rationale
Users frequently hit rcedit pitfalls (path not set, invalid icon, Wine issues) and only discover the root cause by running rcedit manually. Better surfacing reduces support churn and makes CI logs actionable. See #27765, #46218, #57320 for the history and pain points. 
GitHub
+2
GitHub
+2

Implementation (3.x)
In platform/windows/export/... wrap the rcedit invocation with OS::execute(...), capturing exit code and stderr.

Emit WARN_PRINT/ERR_PRINT with a clear prefix (e.g. Windows Export (rcedit): ...).

Pre-flight check: if Modify Resources / app details / icon are set but rcedit path is empty → editor warning with guidance.

Do not use C++17 features (3.x restriction). 
[Godot Engine Documentation](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/contributing/development/cpp_usage_guidelines.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Before / After
Before: Export silently keeps default icon; Wine prints fixme:ver:GetCurrentPackageId ... only if run manually; sometimes misleading “templates missing” error. 
[GitHub](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/28456?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

After:

Editor/CLI shows:

vbnet
Copy
Edit
Windows Export (rcedit): failed with exit code 1
stderr: 014c:fixme:ver:GetCurrentPackageId(...) : stub
Hint: Configure Editor Settings → Export → Windows → rcedit, or disable “Application > Modify Resources”.
When rcedit is missing but fields are set → upfront warning (no crash). (Related evidence in 4.x: #99284.) 
[GitHub](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/99284?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Testing
Linux + Wine: valid rcedit path → icon & PE fields applied, no warnings.

Linux + missing rcedit: fields set → warning; export continues, icon unchanged.

Invalid icon (.ico malformed) → non-zero exit; error surfaced with stderr; export continues without resource modifications.

Windows host without rcedit → clear guidance (no “templates missing”).

CI headless export: non-zero exit code remains visible but no false positives aborting unrelated steps. (See #62874 for context.) 
[GitHub](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/62874?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Docs
Aligned with “Exporting for Windows” / “Changing application icon for Windows” docs that mention rcedit and its configuration. 
Godot Engine Documentation
+1

Related issues
#27765 (this), #57320 (warn when rcedit not configured), #46218 (indication that rcedit is running), #28456 (misleading error when rcedit path invalid). 
GitHub
+3
GitHub
+3
GitHub
+3

Risk/Compatibility
Low. Only export-time behavior; no runtime impact. Strings are new; no API changes.
